beats.prompt: Warm-up — match each birth–death concept to its definition.

pairs.right: move only to a neighbor (nearest-neighbor up/down)
the walk bounces back — a birth–death chain

feedback.correct: A nearest-neighbor up/down walk — gambler's ruin — is a **birth–death** line. Seal the absorbing walls into **reflecting** ends and that same line becomes the canonical **reversible** chain.
hints: Warm-up from Gambler's Ruin — the walk that only steps to a neighbor (P₂ = ½P₃ + ½P₁).
An absorbing walk gets stuck at a wall — but seal the walls to reflect and the same line has a clean long-run π.
Up/down to a neighbor = birth–death; reflect the ends → the canonical reversible chain.

beats.prompt: Two urns, a handful of balls — can you find the long-run distribution π without solving the full πP=π system?

options: No — you must always solve the full πP=π system
Yes — if the chain has no preferred direction, read π off the edges

No — you must always solve the full πP=π.note: Not for a reversible chain. If every edge balances (πᵢpᵢⱼ=πⱼpⱼᵢ) you can walk π out edge by edge — no linear system at all.

Yes — if the chain has no preferred dire.note: Right — detailed balance lets you telescope π edge by edge. On the Ehrenfest urn that's just (1,2,1), normalized to (1/4,1/2,1/4).

Only for tiny 2-state chains.note: No — the shortcut works for any reversible chain, including the m-ball Ehrenfest birth–death line. Size doesn't matter; reversibility does.

hints: No wrong guess yet — commit and we'll test it.
A reversible chain balances every edge, so π telescopes out without the full solve.

beats.prompt: Read this just-in-time primer on reversibility and detailed balance, then continue.

interaction.title: Detailed balance: the movie runs the same backward
interaction.body: A chain is **reversible** when every edge balances its own back-flow: **πᵢpᵢⱼ = πⱼpⱼᵢ**. As much probability crosses i→j as crosses j→i. Film the chain in equilibrium and play it backward — you can't tell the difference. When this holds, you can build π **edge by edge** instead of solving πP=π.

feedback.correct: Detailed balance: **πᵢpᵢⱼ = πⱼpⱼᵢ**. Flow i→j equals flow j→i on every edge — the movie runs the same backward.
hints: Forward flow = back-flow, edge by edge.
Balance is per-edge (πᵢpᵢⱼ=πⱼpⱼᵢ) — stronger than just a steady total.
Reversible ⇒ run the movie backward and it looks identical.

beats.prompt: Given π₀ = 1/4 for the m=2 Ehrenfest urn, apply detailed balance on edge (0,1) to find π₁.

feedback.correct: Detailed balance on edge (0,1): π₀p₀₁ = π₁p₁₀ → (1/4)·1 = π₁·(1/2) → π₁ = **1/2**. One edge, one equation — no system.
hints: Use the edge you just named: π₀p₀₁ = π₁p₁₀.
It's one concrete equation — read p₀₁=1 and p₁₀=1/2 straight off P, plug in π₀=1/4, solve for π₁.

beats.prompt: Step the Ehrenfest urn — watch each crossing 0↔1↔2 happen as often forward as back.

hero.structuralReadout: Each crossing i↔i+1 happens as often forward as back — flows balance edge by edge → π = (1/4, 1/2, 1/4)

feedback.correct: Watch the token cross 0↔1↔2: each crossing happens as often forward as back. Flows balance edge by edge → π = **(1/4, 1/2, 1/4)**.
hints: Step the urn and watch one crossing at a time.
The balls don't drift to a 50/50 split and freeze — they keep crossing; π is Binomial(2,½), not a frozen middle.
Forward flow matches back-flow on every edge → π = (1/4,1/2,1/4).

beats.prompt: Build π via the detailed-balance telescoping product on the m=2 Ehrenfest urn, then normalize.
beats.interviewNote: Detailed balance — πᵢpᵢⱼ=πⱼpⱼᵢ — is the fastest route to a birth–death chain's stationary distribution, the standard follow-up once you've solved πP=π the long way.

feedback.correct: Telescope the edge ratios: π ∝ (1, p₀₁/p₁₀, …) = (1, 2, 1); normalize → **(1/4, 1/2, 1/4)**. No πP=π system — detailed balance built it edge by edge.
hints: Chain the edge balances: π₁/π₀ = p₀₁/p₁₀, then π₂/π₁ = p₁₂/p₂₁.
No hidden system — each ratio is read straight off P: (1, 1/(1/2), …) = (1, 2, 1).

beats.prompt: Three methods all converge on (1/4, 1/2, 1/4) — reveal each lens.

lenses.label: Long-run simulation
lenses.body: Run the urn forever (simulateChain) → fractions 1/4, 1/2, 1/4 = Binomial(2,½)

feedback.correct: Detailed balance, πP=π (L6), and a long-run simulation all land on **(1/4, 1/2, 1/4)** — the same Binomial(2,½), not a trick of one method.
hints: Reveal each lens.
Three roads — edge balance, the full solve, and the simulation — to one π.
All three say (1/4,1/2,1/4).

beats.prompt: Tap edge A→B and its back-edge B→A on the directed 3-cycle — is the chain reversible?

feedback.correct: Directed 3-cycle A→B→C→A: forward flow π_A·p_AB = (1/3)·1 = 1/3, back-flow π_B·p_BA = (1/3)·0 = 0. 1/3 ≠ 0 → detailed balance fails → **not reversible**. It is still *stationary* at (1/3,1/3,1/3) — but not every chain is reversible, so here you must fall back to L6's full πP=π.
hints: Compare forward flow π_A·p_AB with back-flow π_B·p_BA on the A–B edge.
Stationary ≠ reversible. The cycle is stationary at (1/3,1/3,1/3), yet the back-edge B→A has probability 0 — no edge balance.
1/3 ≠ 0 → not reversible.

beats.prompt: Mastery challenge: Ehrenfest urn with m = 3 balls. Find π via the detailed-balance telescoping product.

interaction.scenario: The Ehrenfest urn with \(m = 3\) balls, states \(\{0,1,2,3\}\), transition matrix \(P = [[0,1,0,0],[\tfrac{1}{3},0,\tfrac{2}{3},0],[0,\tfrac{2}{3},0,\tfrac{1}{3}],[0,0,1,0]]\). Find the stationary distribution \(\pi\) via detailed balance.

feedback.correct: Telescope on \(\{0,1,2,3\}\): edge (0,1) gives \(\pi_1 = 3\pi_0\), edge (1,2) gives \(\pi_2 = \pi_1\), edge (2,3) gives \(\pi_3 = \pi_0\) \(\Rightarrow \pi \propto (1,3,3,1)\); normalize by \(2^3 = 8\) \(\Rightarrow \mathbf{(1/8,\, 3/8,\, 3/8,\, 1/8)} = \binom{3}{i}/2^3\). More states, same shortcut — no \(\pi P = \pi\).
hints: Same telescoping product, now four states: \(\pi_1/\pi_0 = p_{01}/p_{10}\), etc.
Still no system — π is Binomial(3,½), not 1/3 again and not a frozen middle: (1,3,3,1).

beats.prompt: Reveal the recap to review the core principle.

feedback.correct: When every edge balances (πᵢpᵢⱼ=πⱼpⱼᵢ), the chain is **reversible** and π telescopes out edge by edge — no πP=π. For a birth–death chain like the Ehrenfest urn that hands you π(i) = C(m,i)/2ᵐ for free.
hints: Reversible = forward flow equals back-flow on every edge.
Telescoping product π ∝ ∏(pₖ,ₖ₊₁/pₖ₊₁,ₖ): Ehrenfest m=2 → (1/4,1/2,1/4), m=3 → (1/8,3/8,3/8,1/8).
When detailed balance fails (a one-way cycle), drop back to L6's full πP=π solve.
